sentences.text: We slept in the Swedish consulate for a, for about a week.
Every night we used to come back there to-- after we finished our task, what they gave us to do.
And we slept there for a while.
But then later on they told us we cannot come back there because it was too obvious, and they're going to get suspicious, we coming and going with the Nazi uniform.
We put on these uniforms, and they send us to these, to these places, which is called the ghettos.
And we had to bring in-- they gave us certain people.
We couldn't take just anybody.
We had to take-- we had to ask for certain people.
And there was the Silber family.
He was a very big Rabbi, his son, and so on.
And all these.
And we should bring these people and hide them out in the basements, in, in the attics.
The basements are so full that they had no more room for the people in the basement.
They used to hide them up in the attics of the building.
Of the Swedish consulate.
It wasn't actually the headquarters of the consulate.
It wasn't the main consulate.
It was like a, a--
An annex of the consulate.

And, and Raoul Wallenberg was there only once a week because there was a lot of people from all kinds of Zionist organizations, from the Mizrachi, and the [NON-ENGLISH], and all different organizations.
They were actually running the show.
And they all had Swedish passports, because they are Swedish citizens and Swiss citizens.
And they are not allowed to be taken because they were on the protection of the neutralization of the Swiss and Swedish consul.
But they gave us mostly the, the orders what to do.
After a week, we had to move out, and we rented a little room from an apartment house.
And we stayed there together, all four boys.
And we slept just in bunks, or on the floor, on carpets, whatever.
Right.
In the beginning, we always wore the Hungarian Nazi uniform.
Later on.
After a while, we went into this-- when we went into the transports, which send us later on to the trains, to the transports to bring out families who were already on the way to Auschwitz.
So he says, you go to the transports.
They gave us all the documents-- documentation papers that we came from the Nazi headquarters.
We have to take these Jews, certain named Jews for questioning in, that we have some suspicion that they know something against the Hungarian regime or the Nazi regime.
And we have to bring them in for questioning.
So we went into these transports.
We brought in-- brought out no more than seven, eight people at a time.
We couldn't take more, because we had a jeep.
And we, of course, everybody was driving.
We knew how to drive.
And we came in, and we told them we have to take these and these Jews.
We gave them certain names, that we have to take them in to questioning, and we'll bring them back to the transports.
Of course, we never brought them back.
We took them out.
And, and they were scared.
They didn't know, those people when we took them from the transport, they didn't know who are, who we are or anything.
So later on when we were driving about a couple miles already from the transports, we told them don't be scared.
It's-- we-- [SOBBING] we are, we are your own people.
We came to try to save you and your families as much as we can.
So they started crying.
And we took them into the Swedish consulate.

And we hid them out there, and we asked them what their needs and if they know anybody, very important people, if they need any help, they should tell us and give us their names and addresses, where they are.
And we're going to try to get them and save them, bring them to either to the Swiss consul or the Swedish consul.
How generally was the knowledge of where the transports went?
Did you know what happened to the people who were transported at that time?
Yeah.
They were all, most of them, all the transports were taken to Auschwitz.
No.
Now.
All we know, they were taken and they just were-- remained in Auschwitz for a while.
And from there, they, they selected people who were strong enough to work for labor.
Who was not strong enough, the old people, women and children, and the weak people, even if they were young.
If they were weak, weren't capable enough to work, they put them in a different transport and they sent them straight to the gas chambers.
Yeah.
We already heard rumors.
We didn't know positively anything.
But we just heard rumors about it.
So we were very determined and bitter to get even for what they do to our people and our families, because all those people were part of our families.
We didn't know-- I didn't know if my mother or father is alive or anybody.
And everybody was my family.
Whoever was Jewish was related to me, because through the Judaism and the Jewish religion.
So we tried to help almost everybody, who were-- but we couldn't show too much because it was very dangerous to show them too much affection in the beginning, because they would give us away.
We were afraid that the people who we tried to save, they might give us away too, because they, you know, they were so done and insensible that they started crying right away.
And they were giving, you know-- so we had to be very cold-hearted in the beginning.
Like, you know, like they were, you know, we want to, we want to, we want to destroy these people.
No.
There were only Nazis, a few Nazis.
Mostly Hungarian Nazis were in charge of these things.
They had prisons full of Jewish people, these Hungarian Nazis.
For no reason at all, they just took them to prison.
They made all kind think stories up.
The Jew is no good, and the Jew is against the Hungarian regime.
They, they incarcerated them or they shipped them out with the, with the transports.
About three months.
And after we came to the transports, the last trip we made-- we must have made there about 15, 16 trips.
But after the 13, 14 trips, they got kind of suspicious on us.
And I thought somebody must have give us away.
And when we came the last time, they were already, the Nazis, the real Nazis, were waiting for us.
The Hungarians.
And there was two Germans and six Hungarian Nazis.
And they were-- they took-- they circled us around, and they had machine guns.
And they started, you know, you are Jews.
You, you are destroying our country, and you are trying to represent us as Nazis.
And it's not-- and we're going to get even with you.
So they lined us up and they handcuffed us.
And they were-- the four Jewish boys, me, and [NON-GERMAN], and [NON-GERMAN], and [NON-GERMAN].
And then also the people who we tried to save, they took them also with us.
So we were about 18 boys and girls, but maybe 8 girls and 10 boys.
And we tried to get out from those transports.
And they, they marched us all over through town, handcuffed, and all of course everybody else.
And they took us to the Danube.
It is a big river.
It's called the Danube.
The Danube.
The Danube.
The Danube.
They marched as to the Danube, and they lined us up against the edge of the water.
And three Nazis, Hungarians, had machine guns.
And one-- and a couple guys had only pistols.
And they started, they started shooting at everybody.
They didn't even look.
Just keep shooting, like, like wurst.
And then the treated us like, like, I don't know.
It was so terrible.
So people, when they shot them, they fell in the water.
They fell in the Danube and they drowned, and who knows what happened to them.
So they started shooting and I seen everybody's falling.
And a guy fell on top of me.
And I fell in the water too, but I was shot only in the leg and in the thigh.
I stayed under the water for about a few minutes.
And I started to move around under the water and go towards-- there was brushes, you know, like growth from the water come out, growth.
And I went to some growth and held on, and I tried to get a little air.
And I stayed under the water for two, three hours until it got dark.
I was afraid to come out because they also had dogs with them.
When I, when I heard everything quieted down and there was nobody moving, nobody-- everybody was dead-- I don't know how I really-- it was a miracle from God that I survived.
I don't know.
I was the only person from the 18 people who survived those shootings and came up from the water.
Most of them must have drowned and got killed.
After I came out from the water--
Yeah, I was still handcuffed.
And I came out from the water.
And I try to loosen up with my hands, but very narrow and small.
So I, I pulled out my right arm.
In my left arm I still had the handcuffs on.
But somehow I just slipped through because it was wet and greasy from the, from the oil or something.
And I was able to loosen my right arm.
After that, I somehow managed to get my handcuffs off.
And I was kind of so sick I couldn't walk.
I just called on my hands and feet for-- in the beginning.
I couldn't stand up.
Yeah, I was wounded.
And I couldn't stand up, but I was crawling.
And I came to a place to where there was a little island there.
And I stayed there for about a couple hours.
And I, I heard the, the like a train going and like cars running.
So I started going in the noise, the direction of the noise from the, from the cars and trains.
And I came to a street, and I see-- I says to a guy there-- was people walking already.
Was early morning, maybe 5 o'clock in the morning.
And people started walking to work.
And I speak good Hungarian.
I says to the guy, listen, I just came from the front, from the, from the-- fighting the Russians.
I says, I'm one-- I'm a freedom fighter for the Hungarian freedom fighters.
Can you direct me to a first aid station?
I was shot in my thighs and my legs.
And I got hurt, but I could hardly talk.
I was stuttering, more stuttering than talking clearly.
And I was kind of very, very bad off.
And he says, yeah, if you go down three streets to the right, there is a first aid station for the, for the soldiers.
Sure enough, I came three, four streets down.
And I saw the big red cross there.
And it says, first aid station.
So I came into that first aid station.
And I says, I just came from the, from the, from the front.
And we were wounded.
And there's quite a few Hungarian boys who got hurt and killed.
And I just crawled in here to get first aid.
And the first aid station started asking me questions.
And I, I couldn't speak.
I got so scared that I lost my voice.
My voice just completely disappeared.
And I says, first give me first aid, and then later on I'll tell you the story.
So they gave me bandages.
And it was not much first aid there.
They just try to bandage you up.
You shouldn't bleed more than you were bleeding.
And the, the first aid station was so full of soldiers, men and women, that there was no room where to go.
Everything, even the hallways, people were laying in the hallways.
The front was only about three kilometers.
They were fighting.
The Russians were already on the, on the outskirts of Buda, Buda and Pest.
They were on the outskirts.
But the problem was, they wouldn't give up, the Hungarians.
They were fighting from building to building.
They were coming through the roofs.
From one building to the other building, they were coming into the roofs.
In 1944 in, in April.
In April.
March, April, May already.
Right.
Was in 1944, right.
No, the Russians didn't get to until much later, in 1945, probably.
They occupied the city in 1945, yes.
But they were already on the outskirts in 1944 there.
End of 1944, was winter time maybe.
Could have been-- I don't remember, you know, the dates and the days or years.
It could have been, because it was snow on the ground.
So it must have been already the winter time.
They were already on the outskirts of Buda and Pest.
But they couldn't take the city, so they take only parts of the city.
And certain-- they came from building to building.
At the first aid station, I was there for just one day.
And when night came, I told them I have to leave because I have to go in the bathroom.
So there was no bathrooms, was terrible.
But the bathrooms used to be somewhere outside.
Not in the same building.
You had to go outside to like an outhouse.
So I walked out there, and I says, I'll be back.
I just go in the bathroom, and I'll be back.
And they didn't, they didn't think I was suspicious, so they didn't watch after me.
So I walked out, and I went back after I walked out.
I was bandaged up.
I went back, somehow found out where the Swedish consul is and the, and the direction.
And I went back there.
And when I came back there, nobody believed it that I'm still alive, because they heard.
The rumor went around that we got captured and we got all executed.
And when I came back-- so everybody was looking at me like a dead man walking.
He says, how come you're alive?
How come you got away?
I says, I don't know how come, but I just, I got shot.
But I got away.
And everybody-- I lost my best friends.
They all died there.
And also those people, the names who gave us, they all got executed.
And I imagine nobody survived.
I don't know if anybody came back, but I'm here.
And I started crying, and they started crying.
And they gave me some food.
And I stayed there for three days and three nights in the basement hidden, because I was very weak and very scared and very badly hurt.

And after I got a little better, they told me, Harry, if you're feeling strong enough, then you can go out and see if you can get some food for these people, because everybody is starving from hunger.
Those people weren't allowed to go out shopping or anything, and you couldn't get food.
So I says, yeah, I think I'll be all right.
So I got dressed--
Yeah.
I put on the same thing.
And I says, if I have to die, I have to die.
I says, if God will, will save me, I will be alive.
I put on that Hungarian uniform and I went out to town.
And I came to a bakery, a big bakery.
And they were baking all kind breads and rolls.
And I says to them, listen, I need to have a certain amount of bread and rolls for people there, there in the hospital.
And they're wounded people from our own people.
And of course the Swedish consul had money.
And they gave me money to buy this stuff.
So they sold it to me, and was in, in big sacks.
Hard bread thrown in, about 30, 40 loaves of bread.
And I used to carry it on my back and carry it back to the Swedish consul.
But I have to always watch behind me if anybody follows me, because you were always afraid.
You know, it's a dead giveaway.
How come you're carrying all that bread and all that?
You used to smell-- you could smell the bread.
You know, fresh bread baked, you can smell.
And thanks God, I got away with it.
And I brought the bread in and everybody was so happy.
And they distributed the bread, the bread to everybody a piece, not even a whole bread.
Just a quarter of a bread, or a couple slices.
I stayed there for about three, four days, and I did that.
And the same thing I did with the grocery stores.
I used to go and get some food, as much as I could get.
And later on, it got so bad that you couldn't go out to the street anymore.
Because they were watching every move people made from the consulate.
So I had to go out through different roads, through the back, and you were afraid.
So I never went back there again.
And I took off that Hungarian uniform.
I put on plain clothes people.
And I went into Budapest, and, and it was like called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like a secondhand store.
They sell all kinds of things there, clothes, and shoes, and even food, like a market.
It was close to the train station.
It's a huge place.
I was there for a while, and I walk around, and all of a sudden a Hungarian guy, a Christian guy, recognized me.
And he started screaming, oh, this guy is a Jew.
And I got scared.
See, it's a dead giveaway.
They're going to catch me again, and I'm going to be in a lot of trouble.
So I started running.
And as I was running, towards me came two ladies.
And I don't know, I never seen them.
Accidentally I must have knocked them over and they fell.
And I kept-- I didn't even look back.
I heard them fell.
And I kept running.
And I seen people were chasing me.
And I kept-- I was very, very scared, and I was running like a wild man.
So I didn't even see where I'm running.
All of a sudden, I came and I seen the train is moving out of the station.
So I jumped on the train, and the train moved out.
And I was on the train, and I was looking where the end of the train, I was going way towards the end of the train.
And I look around, and I see nobody bothering me, nobody's looking at me.
So before the train even came into the next station, I jumped off because I was afraid that they telephoned the station in the train that they should look for me.
There's a guy is hiding on the train.
He's a Jew or something.
So I jumped off the train.
And I came off the train and there were already on the end of the train some barracks with some Russian soldiers.
And I went over there to the Russian soldiers and I started telling them, [NON-ENGLISH], I'm a Jew, and I'm a comrade.
I'm a friend.
And they didn't want to know nothing.
[NON-ENGLISH], no, no, no.
I says, I says, I've suffered enough.
Can't you help me?
They wouldn't offer you no help or nothing whatsoever.
And I had a nice leather jacket and a nice pair of shiny boots.
So they divide, divide.
They tore off my leather jacket and my boots from my feet.
And they locked me-- and they-- I said, give me my shoes back.
So they took the machine gun and says, if you don't go, we're going to shoot you.
So I said, to hell with the boots and the jacket, and I kept going.
So later on, I found out another transport people with Russians, and they threw me in the line.
I says, what's going on here?
He says, those are prisoners of war.
Those are soldiers, prisoners of war.
And they were a lot of Jewish boys there.
And they took them as prisoners of war.
I says, well, they're not prisoners.
They're not prisoners of war.
They're Jewish people.
They're trying to escape from the Nazis.
But the Russians didn't want to listen or heard anything.
And I speak Russian and also Hungarian and Czechoslovakian.
But they were kind of very anti-Semitic in the beginning.
And they didn't want to know nothing.
[NON-ENGLISH].
So I says to them, OK.
So they threw me in the line.
And I was walking with the, with the, with the prisoners of war for a while.
And all of a sudden we stopped in a depot, in a train depot.
And there was a lot of trains lined up.
One going left, one going right, backwards and forwards.
And so they made everybody carry stuff on the train.
There were sacks of flour, big sacks of flour.
I think they weighed about 80 kilos.
And I wasn't more than-- I don't think I weighed 80 kilos.
So they made you carry it from one transport to the other, these, that flours.
And it was snowing, and it was cold, and the snow was wet.
We carried the flours.
And I got dough all over myself from the powder, from the sacks came out the powder from the flour.
And I was dough all over my face, my ears, my eyes.
And I says, that's no life.
I don't want to wind up that way.
Finally I'm escaping the Nazis, here I'm getting in worse trouble with the Russians.
So the next day, they put us back marching again, and when they start matching, I marched-- I start marching with them.
And I seen we're going through a wooded area, so I hid out behind a piece of, you know, one of those trees.
Good, and I hid there, and when everybody matched away, I started marching in the other direction.
I was lucky.
I says, my God, they're going to take me now to another labor camp?
The Russians and the Siber someplace.
Sure enough, they took [NON-ENGLISH].
They took a lot of Jewish boys I recognized there.
And they took them to, to Siber.
I remained behind, and then I started taking trains.
I seen all these transports going back east towards the Carpathian.
And there were Russian trains, Russian soldiers on it.
So I jumped up and I started to, you know, clean myself up.
And I says, I'm going to try to go back to my hometown and see if anybody's alive, anybody came home from the concentration camps or the labor camps.
Sure enough, I came back to Mukach--
It was 1945.
I came back to Mukach--
In the war, they were still shooting here and there, but it wasn't completely over.
But it was kind of quieted down, especially in my part of the world, you know.
In the Carpathian, was no more-- they were occupied by the Russians.
And you used to be called SSSR.
Used to belong to Russia already.
[NON-ENGLISH].
The Russians had annexed it.
Yeah, the Russian occupied it, and they said it belongs to Russia already.
And I came to town, and it was very early in the morning, about 4 o'clock or 5 o'clock in the morning.
It was kind of still a little dark and daylight started coming out.
And there was very few people on the street.
And I seen some truck go by, some buses.
I says, hey, is there any place here around where there is some people came back to town, some Jewish people?
He says, yeah we heard some Jews came back, and they lived there, and they.
So I went first back to the house where my father used to own.
A big-- we used to own a big apartment complex with four apartments on each side and three front-- the front was three big stores.
So I come back there.
And everything was closed up, and they wouldn't let me in.
And I don't think they knew who I am or recognized me.
So I says, did anybody came back?
Any of my family, my brothers?
Oh, we don't know.
We don't know.
And was kind of-- Ukrainers there already, occupied.
They took over the whole complex with all the furniture and everything, because they left everything behind.
So it was nothing I could claim in the beginning.
So then I found out later in the day, I started walk around town.
My brother came home for you.
He was in the Ukraine in the Ukraine labor camps.
And he came home.
And I found out that he's alive.
And he came home, and he lives with three other Jewish boys.
So it was something.
